ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for white bread with kaya and peanut butter; mapped to the closest parent class 'bread food product'. Kaya (coconut egg jam) and peanut butter toppings are not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for white bread with kaya and peanut butter as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Singaporean bread dish.

serving.description: 2 slices of white bread with kaya and peanut butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two slices of white bread (approximately 100 g total) spread with kaya and peanut butter, commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with kaya and peanut butter — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'White bread with kaya and peanut butter' (cr_id F05020129). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Bread — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to white bread with kaya and peanut butter composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: White bread has a well-established GI of approximately 71 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Kaya (coconut egg jam) adds refined sugar which maintains or elevates the glycaemic response. Peanut butter contributes fat and protein which may modestly attenuate the glycaemic response, but the dominant carbohydrate matrix is white bread. GI of 71 is applied as a conservative analogous estimate for this composite dish. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (71) × available carbohydrate per serving (43.82 g per 100 g × 1.0 serving of 100 g) / 100 = 31.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 381 mg/100 g with low potassium (110 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.81 g/100 g); sugar from kaya (8.59 g/100 g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this dish to no more than one slice (approximately 50 g), choose natural peanut butter without added salt to reduce sodium intake, and consider replacing kaya with a lower-sugar spread to reduce the combined sodium and sugar burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index white bread (GI ~71) combined with kaya sugar (8.59 g/100 g total sugar) and low dietary fibre (2.81 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 31 per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, portion should not exceed 50 g, and substitution with wholegrain bread and a lower-sugar spread is strongly recommended to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 381 mg/100 g is elevated; cholesterol at 16.69 mg/100 g from egg-based kaya is present; fat at 10.81 g/100 g includes saturated fat from coconut in kaya, though peanut butter contributes beneficial MUFA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sodium intake from this dish by reducing portion size and choosing unsalted natural peanut butter; the saturated fat from coconut-based kaya should be moderated, and wholegrain bread substitution is preferred to improve the fibre and lipid profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate from white bread and refined sugar from kaya promote pro-inflammatory mediators; coconut-derived saturated fat in kaya is pro-inflammatory; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this combination; if bread is consumed, substitute wholegrain bread, replace kaya with a polyphenol-rich spread, and use natural peanut butter without added sugar to reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with kaya and peanut butter is a classic Singaporean breakfast and snack dish consisting of sliced white bread spread with kaya — a sweet coconut and egg jam flavoured with pandan — and peanut butter. Kaya is a distinctly Southeast Asian condiment made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaves, giving it a rich, creamy, mildly sweet flavour with a subtle coconut aroma. Peanut butter adds a nutty, savoury-sweet contrast and contributes protein and monounsaturated fat. The combination is commonly served at traditional Singaporean kopitiams (coffee shops) alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (tea). Nutritionally, this dish provides 315 kcal per 100 g, with 11.26 g protein, 10.81 g fat, and 43.82 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 381 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is high (~71) due to the white bread base and added sugar from kaya, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension without strict portion control. Dietary fibre is modest at 2.81 g/100 g. The dish is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains gluten, eggs, and peanuts as major allergens. It is best consumed in moderation as an occasional breakfast treat rather than a daily staple.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F05020129) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match to an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread as the analogous food, which is the dominant carbohydrate matrix.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100 g based on typical composition of white bread with kaya and peanut butter using USDA FoodData Central component data as a supplementary reference; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this composite dish, and no sufficiently close approved-source equivalent was available to supply these values without unacceptable estimation risk. Potassium (110 mg/100 g), calcium (162 mg/100 g), iron (4.34 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (16.69 mg/100 g) are reported directly by HPB. All core macros (protein_g 11.26, fat_g 10.81, carbohydrate_g 43.82) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; the peanut butter component may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response but the white bread matrix dominates. Dietary tag 'plant_based' from input has been replaced with 'vegetarian' as kaya contains eggs, making the dish not fully plant-based/vegan.
